name: Numerical Methods
description: Finite element methods (FEM), Monte Carlo simulation, numerical integration, finite difference schemes, iterative solvers, spectral methods, and uncertainty quantification. The mathematics of computing approximate solutions to problems that are analytically intractable — the bridge from pure math to real computation. Every numerical solution has error, and understanding, bounding, and controlling that error is the core discipline. Depends on 444 (algebra), 500 (calculus), 533 (analysis via stability), 566 (linear algebra via matrix solvers), 650 (discrete math via meshes), 666 (computation via algorithm complexity), and 699 (optimization via numerical optimization). Terminal node in the math dependency tree.
axioms: Error decomposition: total error = discretization error + round-off error + statistical error
Stability: a method is stable if small input errors produce small output errors
Convergence: the numerical solution approaches the true solution as discretization parameters refine (Lax equivalence theorem: consistency + stability → convergence)
Lax-Richtmyer equivalence theorem: for well-posed linear PDEs, consistency + stability ⇔ convergence
Monte Carlo error scales as O(1/√N) — dimension-independent but slow
Courant-Friedrichs-Lewy (CFL) condition: time step must respect grid spacing for explicit PDE solvers
Condition number κ(A) = ||A||·||A⁻¹|| bounds the sensitivity of linear system solutions
reasoning_patterns: error bound estimation and convergence rate analysis
Monte Carlo sampling and variance reduction (importance sampling, antithetic variates)
finite element discretization: variational formulation, basis functions, element assembly
finite difference schemes and truncation error analysis
stiffness detection and appropriate solver choice
uncertainty quantification and confidence intervals on numerical outputs
boundary_conditions: All numerical solutions have error — it must be bounded and reported
Stiff ODEs require implicit methods — explicit methods are unstable
Monte Carlo converges at 1/√N — universal but slow; quasi-Monte Carlo helps for low dimensions
High-dimensional integration suffers the curse of dimensionality (except Monte Carlo)
Ill-conditioned problems (high condition number) amplify input errors catastrophically
Mesh generation for complex 3D geometries is a major practical bottleneck
Parallel scaling is limited by Amdahl's law for serial portions of the algorithm
